ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_03301103 covers roasted coffee beans as a category; no specific French Roast sub-entry exists. Used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers roasted coffee generically; no French Roast dark-roast specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34731007 is the substance-level code for coffee; no roast-level specificity available.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of ground coffee is approximately 7 g (one tablespoon), used to brew one 180–240 ml cup.

nutrient_source.title: Coffee, brewed from grounds, prepared with tap water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #14209 / Ground roasted coffee beans USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are reported per 100 g of dry ground roasted coffee (unbrewed). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 14209 (brewed coffee) and entry 14201 (dry instant coffee) were used as cross-references. Values for dry ground roasted coffee are adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB 14214 (Coffee, dry, powder) and peer-reviewed composition tables for roasted Arabica/Robusta blends. French Roast is an extra-dark roast; the roasting process reduces moisture and slightly alters chlorogenic acid content but does not materially change macronutrient profile relative to standard roasted ground coffee.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of coffee and coffee-based beverages — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ground roasted coffee (dry, unbrewed) contains negligible available carbohydrates and has no measurable glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database lists brewed black coffee as having a GI of effectively zero (no glycemic carbohydrates). Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) to satisfy numeric field requirements. gi_category is low. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because available carbohydrate per 7 g serving is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (13 mg per 100 g dry grounds); caffeine may cause transient blood pressure elevation
hypertension.guidance: Ground French Roast coffee is very low in sodium and poses minimal dietary sodium concern for hypertensive individuals; however, caffeine intake should be moderated to 200–300 mg per day as caffeine can transiently raise blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible available carbohydrates and zero glycemic load; chlorogenic acids may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black French Roast coffee contains no significant available carbohydrates and has a glycemic load of zero per serving, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar or high-fat creamers.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat; contains diterpenes (cafestol, kahweol) if unfiltered; moderate caffeine
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Filtered French Roast coffee is low in saturated fat and cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should use paper-filtered brewing methods to minimise exposure to cafestol and kahweol, which can raise LDL cholesterol when unfiltered.

llm_training.natural_language_description: French Roast Ground Coffee is an extra-dark roasted ground coffee product sold by Starbucks in a 250 g retail pack. French Roast represents one of the darkest roast profiles available commercially, characterised by a smoky, bold, and intensely bitter flavour with low acidity. The beans are roasted until the second crack, producing a dark brown to near-black colour and a shiny oily surface due to the migration of coffee oils to the bean surface. The product is sold as pre-ground coffee suitable for drip, French press, or espresso brewing. Per 100 g of dry ground coffee, it provides approximately 331 kcal, 12.2 g protein, 1.7 g fat, and 55 g total carbohydrate (of which approximately 24 g is dietary fibre and 0 g is available sugar). The high fibre content reflects the insoluble cell-wall polysaccharides in the coffee grounds, which are not extracted into the brewed beverage. When brewed as black coffee, the resulting beverage contains negligible macronutrients and has a glycemic index effectively of zero. French Roast coffee is rich in chlorogenic acids, which are polyphenolic antioxidants associated with improved insulin sensitivity and reduced cardiovascular risk in epidemiological studies. It is also a significant source of niacin (vitamin B3) and potassium. The product is vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, black French Roast coffee is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or cream, and caffeine intake is kept within recommended limits.
common_user_queries: How many calories in French Roast coffee?
Is dark roast coffee good for diabetics?
What is the GI of French Roast coffee?
How much caffeine in French Roast?
Is French Roast coffee low carb?
Can I drink French Roast coffee if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for dry roasted ground coffee (NDB 14214 and related entries). No exact USDA entry for Starbucks French Roast 250 g ground coffee was used as primary source; values represent the standard composition of dark-roasted ground Arabica/Robusta coffee. The product-specific nutritional label was not available from an approved source, so USDA SR Legacy data for roasted ground coffee was used as the closest approved match.
notes: 1. This record covers the dry ground coffee product (250 g retail pack), not the brewed beverage. Nutrient values are per 100 g of dry ground coffee. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for roasted coffee; naturally zero by product composition. 3. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; roasted coffee contains no free sugars. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived product. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; chlorogenic acids are destroyed during roasting and vitamin C is not present in roasted coffee. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for roasted coffee. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived product. 8. folate_mcg_dfe is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for roasted ground coffee. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; coffee contains no vitamin D. 10. vitamin_e_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for roasted ground coffee. 11. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this product contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 per 7 g serving. 12. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg are all sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for roasted ground coffee. 13. This food is classified under Beverages food group as it is a coffee product intended for beverage preparation. 14. Multilingual local names are not applicable for this product as French Roast coffee is an internationally traded commodity of French/European origin, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish requiring mandatory local-language names under the Veritas multilingual rules. The cuisine category is Coffee (not a cuisine requiring mandatory local-language names under G6). 15. Price information from field survey data (SGD 17.70) is preserved in source metadata but not included in the nutrient record as it is not a nutritional field.

improvement_actions_taken: Set glycemic_index to 1 (sentinel value) per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule; documented in notes and gi_source.

Documented all zero values (trans_fat_g, sugar_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) with confirmed zero or naturally zero justification phrases in notes per G4 zero-macro justification rule.
Verified aliases_by_language entries are all present verbatim in flat aliases list per Q8 aliases consistency rule.
Used closest_parent match_type for all three ontology anchors per G5 ontology match_type rule; no invented codes.
Confirmed G6 multilingual compliance: Coffee cuisine does not require mandatory local-language names; local_names fields set to null with explanation in notes.
